that this is a special treat. First of all, this guy doesn't come out all that often. And so I'm thrilled to have him here. But for those of you who don't know, this is Rich Sheffrin, the founder of Strategic Profits. This is who is referred to as the guru to the gurus because most of the biggest names in online marketing have either gone through his coaching or have come to him for consulting. All of the biggest names, at some point or another, have gone to this guy. This guy has worked with Yahoo. This guy has been paid by Google. This guy has regularly paid a lot of money for a single day's worth of consulting. And so this is a tremendous treat to have. He's a great friend of my dear, dear friend. He's a mentor to me. And I don't believe that I would be in the spot that I am today if it wasn't for my relationship and friendship with him. And so please do me a favor very quickly and give this guy a little... Cool. So what we're going to talk about with Rich is I mentioned earlier, this is a guy who's got an incredible knack for coming up with connections and seeing connections, making connections, seeing relationships. And he's leveraged that skill and that ability for multiple millions of dollars. And so we're going to talk about how he's been able to do that and how he came up with these like his top three best promotions, how he came up with the ideas behind them. I also want this to be interactive. So I want you to, if you've got questions as we're going through this, raise your hand and let's make sure that we dig deep. I don't want this to be just a superficial talk. I've asked this guy questions. I can ask him questions anytime. This is for you. And so make sure that if there's an area or something that I'm bringing up with him that if you want to go deeper on, this is our opportunity to do that. Okay? So let's start with the promo man that put you on the map. And so the internet business manifesto. First of all, tell these guys how many downloads has that thing been... Reportedly it's... Over two million at this point. Yeah. One PDF over two million downloads. And how long was the PDF? Was what? 29? 29 pages. And so and that one PDF launched, it generated, if you will share, millions of dollars within... Three and a half million in the first like month and a half, but probably like 20 or 30 million in total. It's been like that. One PDF, right? Show you the power of... Now, at the root of the PDF was this idea of being a strategic entrepreneur versus being an opportunity seeker. And so just talk about a little bit about how did your mind even come to identifying the problem in the marketplace? Like... I got it. Yeah. So like to go back in time a little bit even before that. I had a really bad business deal that blew up on me. So much so that I had like a lot of rage. And I went to see like a psychotherapist for a while to get over that rage. And I got over that rage. And when the session... When my sessions were done with this guy, he said, you know, I think you might have ADD. You might want to explore that. And I was like, there's no way I have ADD because I tend to hyper focus. So I just kind of... Just like went right past me. But like two years later, I was in a bookstore. And I started just like skimming, driven to distraction. A popular book on ADD, probably like the first big popular book on ADD. And in that book, as I was skimming through it, like there were so many of my characteristics that it described. And I'm like, oh my God, this is me. And so I brought it home. I read it really quickly. And I was convinced I had ADD. And I did. And I gave it to my wife. And I was like, you need to read this. Tell me what you think. And she's like, oh, this describes you. And there was a moment though, when I was reading that book, where like I... This big epiphany happened for me, like I have ADD. And there was like this moment of release. Like I had been struggling with like all these issues of being flaky, procrastinating, like all this kind of stuff. And in that moment, when I like thought I had ADD, like it gave me newfound hope that like all of a sudden my life could be better. It kind of explained away all the problems that I had up until that point. And in that moment, like it was profound, right? Like that understanding of where my problems were really coming from, why I hadn't been able to solve them. And now maybe there was a path to solve them. And so that is kind what I attempt to do when I'm writing a report. And I've kind of lost that way sometimes after the manifesto. But the manifesto, it was clear that that's what I was trying to really create in the minds of the people reading it. That same experience, but not related to ADD, related to the current predicament. So I was taking some notes as I was sitting here. And there's a couple of like core thoughts that I have that kind of I bring with me when I'm doing this kind of stuff. And the first is that the market is made up of frustrated prospects. Like the idea that you're talking to a market, what constitutes a market is that they have an interest. And if they already solved it, like they solved the problem that they were struggling with, they got the goal that they were trying to attain, where they got rid of the problem that got them in the market in the first place, they'd no longer be in the market. So the fact that they're in the market means that they've attempted something. And if they're still in the market, which is who you're talking to, then they're frustrated with the outcome. They haven't gotten the outcome that they wanted. And something that Todd and I have talked a lot about is Gene Schwartz's book Breakthrough Advertising. And on the very first page, he talks about how you cannot channel desire, desire either exists or it doesn't exist. But you can, I mean, you can't create desire, you can channel desire, you can't create desire. Desire either exists or doesn't exist, but you can channel desire into demand for your product. So we're dealing with a group of frustrated people with some outcome who have a desire, like whatever the end goal is. So like when I wrote the manifesto, who I was writing to, were internet marketers, people who were trying to make money online, right? That was the frustration that they weren't getting all the money that they wanted online or they probably wouldn't be reading the manifesto. But they've attempted numerous things and they've been frustrated. So the goal is, when I look at it, like when I approach it is, how do I channel the desire they already have for that outcome through my product? Like, you know, they have a current way of doing it. They have a current belief about what needs to occur for them to get it. But how can I kind of insert myself into that thought process? And so what I've always done, which also goes back to Gene Schwartz, is attempt to bring a new mechanism to the market, right? The idea being that they're, you know, it's kind of like the stages of awareness, have you gone over that or went over that. So I don't think there's any market that's really in a stage one or a stage two. Like in our current reality, like most people are somewhat skeptical, that you're not just going to come out with some claim and it's going to be bought into. And in stage three, you have to bring a new mechanism to the market. And if you get like the concept that everybody's frustrated with the outcome, then that really opens up the space to introduce a new mechanism, a new way of achieving that outcome, right? So when you do that, when you introduce a new mechanism, what I try to do is make the problems that are currently being experienced a symptom. Like these are all now symptoms of the old mechanism, if that makes sense. Huge. What he just said, make sure that you make note of that. So any problems that they're experiencing are being caused by the way that they've been approaching trying to get the outcome, right? So like the U diagram and the internet business manifesto was all about how going out and getting more marketing tactics is causing you to become more distracted, more unfocused, more making things more complicated than they need to be, etc, etc, etc. So I've made the old solution the genesis of the problem, right? Which gives, if you get it, like what that does when effective is that it gets people to realize that it's not them. Like there's nothing inherently wrong with them. That what's wrong is the approach they've been taking, right? Getting all, like I've always found it cheesy like in copy when I read it's not your fault. I'm trying to get people to come to that conclusion, right? Because just someone saying it, what does that really mean? But if they can come to that conclusion, it's really powerful. Like I've been attempting to go about this the completely wrong way. And that, they still need a way. But in that moment, they've just detached from the way that they believe they needed to get it and what, and everything that's been sold to them so far. So you create it like a rift. It provides new hope, right? Just like I had new hope when I read that ADD book, all of a sudden there's a new way that I haven't thought of before to get the outcome. It gets them off the hook, which I said, and hopefully creates an epiphany that they get it. Like, oh, I get it. I see it. Now I realize now, like all of a sudden, everything looks different for me. Just like I had with reason. You see a couple things guys. Do you see the first of all intellectual stimulation? I see it. I get it. It makes sense to me. And also, you know, Rich talking about that they identify the rich repositioned their experience as a symptom of what he can solve. Pretty powerful. And I think that you don't have to look far, like, to see examples of this, even outside of marketing stuff. A couple of books, as I was thinking about it just this morning, the E-Mith was a book like that, right, that got people to all of a sudden say, oh, it's a system that's a problem. Another book, Getting Things Done. Getting Things Done, like I believe part of the popularity was, he was saying like the to-do list is part of the problem. Like the way you currently are structuring it, it doesn't make any sense for this day and age, right? You need the separate list based on context, based on next actions. You can't think of projects and think about doing a project. You have to think about doing a task. So he took aim at what the common current solution was, and that made like his new solution seem all that much more powerful. Another book is The Now Habit, if you've read it. The Now Habit takes like an anti-scheduling approach. The idea is that you only schedule your fun activities on your calendar, and then you rush to get your other work done, because procrastinators usually do the opposite. And The Now Habit has like a cult following too. And all these books, what they do is they kind of assert that the current way of doing things is part of the problem. And, you know, like I said, it's all based on the idea that what makes up the market is a bunch of frustrated people who haven't gotten the outcome that they wanted. Let me just say one other thing too, subtle point, but to make note of what Rich said. One of the common things with those examples that Rich gave, and this is a deeper topic, but it's worth you guys noting, is the difference between selling improvement or enhancement and selling a new thing, a new opportunity. And so, you know, like getting things done, you know, that's an example of a new system. It's not here's a way to become more productive or here's a way to get better at time management. This is a new method, right, a new process. And so, if you look at the, and we're going to talk about this in the last session of the day, but if you look at the six-figure funnel formula, the funnel for that, which is just continuing to crank with cold traffic, and you'll see I'm going to walk you guys through some of that. It's not here's how to improve your current conversions. It's a whole new method. It's a whole new process. It's the difference between improvement or, you know, what's sometimes referred to as repair or enhancement, and a whole new opportunity, right, a whole new way of going about X. Yeah, and just something that I would add to what you said just now and also in relation to what you were saying in the last section about research. I'm not like an overly metaphysical type person, but what I do believe is that reality is a lot more complex than what appears, right, that like we assign kind of cause and effect, but that generally there's a lot more causes going on that we're just not even aware of. So what I'm looking for when I'm doing research are not necessarily answers. I'm looking for clues that back up like the belief that what people are thinking is the way to get the solution is not really the way to get the solution, right. So I'm looking, I'm not looking for evidence of that. I'm looking for clues of it because I'll like I'll connect the evidence later. It's kind of, you know, when you go to a crime scene, like I love forensic files, the TV show forensic files, you know, it starts with like a murder or something horrible and the, you know, the detectives come, but they're not looking for like the evidence at that point, although some of it will turn into evidence. They're looking for clues. They're looking for like what has occurred here that I can use to, you know, to track down the person and to figure out what's happened. And so I'm looking for clues that the current way of doing things isn't the ideal way, right. And then that kind of focuses my information gathering like addiction as it relates to finding evidence to kind of support the big idea. Even if I don't have the big idea yet, I'm kind of approaching it with just the idea that the current way of looking at it, the current way of solving it, isn't the right way. And I know I have an audience for that because they haven't been able to get the outcome. What this does, and then like I'll circle it back to the Internet business manifesto, is basically you're defining a new problem. You're taking like the current problems that exist in a marketplace and now saying these are all symptoms of a deeper problem, right, which relates back to the manifesto, but I've done it a couple of times outside of that, which gives you the power of a big idea, but it also gives you a marketplace of one because all of a sudden you're solving the real problem. Everyone else is kind of contributing to the problem. Yeah, let me spell this out for you. For those of you that maybe haven't read the Internet business manifesto, so Rich mentioned the U diagram. So the U diagram was this really powerful illustration that had you at the center and virtually dozens and dozens of tasks and tactics and responsibilities of the typical online entrepreneur at the time. So the online entrepreneur was very overwhelmed and overworked and disappointed and frustrated. And while there were other people that were talking about other ways of dealing with that overwhelm, other ways of dealing with that, Rich came along and said getting better at dealing with this, that's not the issue. The issue really is, right, the real problem is that you're being an opportunity seeker and not a strategic entrepreneur. And only until you become a strategic entrepreneur will you no longer experience this overwhelm. Now keep in mind, Rich's coaching program was all about how to become a strategic entrepreneur, which is what made it brilliant, right, oddly enough. Hi, I wonder where I can learn this. But do you understand that? Like that, you know, there was this problem that folks were dealing with and in their mind that was their problem. How do I figure out how to best juggle all these activities? And Rich came along and he said, no, no, no, that's not your problem. That's a symptom of your problem. The real problem is this, that you're being an opportunity seeker and not a strategic entrepreneur. And at the time, nobody had said that before. Nobody ever, you know, like I had never heard that. And Rich will share, I'm sure, how he kind of, how he stumbled on that and used that idea. But then he created this whole new paradigm, right, at the time, which was I can no longer be an opportunity seeker, which to this day, people still talk about and it's still on my mind. But see how he recreated, repositioned the problem. And he did so many different things along the way, like the, to the prospect, he was the first guy to really point that out to them. He was the guy that could better articulate their problem better than they could even. And so he repositioned this whole thing, of course, repositioned it in a way that, as we're going to talk about in just a bit, set up the sale, right? You know, you, when you learn something new, a new discovery, the new discovery for me at the time, because by the way, I paid him, I paid him several thousand dollars to be a client. From the manifesto. From the manifesto, yeah. And, and it was, again, like I said earlier, logically, wow, I get it, that makes absolute total sense, right? It makes total sense. All the emotion was in there because it was like, yeah, I don't want to be overwhelmed. I don't want to have this feeling of frustration as a woman. I want to, you know, have, so right there was the emotional peel. And of course, he, you know, he was the guy. He was the guy to learn that from, if for no other reason then, because he was the only guy that ever articulated the problem that way. He was the guy because the source of my, aha, was him. And so, of course, it was, there was no question in my mind, like, he's the Mac Daddy. And, and so, yeah. And so part of it is, you know, like Todd was talking about in the research section about how, you know, what is it that these guys know or doing that I'm not doing that struggle? You know, and I had that same, same thought that I struggled with years and years before the manifesto. But one thing that's, especially if you're talking to your market, but even if you're not, that which is most personal is most general. So the things that you, that torment you, that you struggle with are the same things that your prospect is tormented by and struggles with. And more often than not, those things are not articulated. And you don't ever have to, you know, I go back and forth. Sometimes I'll admit that these were my thoughts. A lot of times I don't. I just describe it to the marketplace, like you might be experiencing this or experiencing that. And one of the pieces of feedback that I consistently got on all my reports is that it feels like you're standing right over my shoulder, like that you know what I'm going through, what I'm experiencing. And it's not because, like I'm clairvoyant or anything like that. It's just that I was willing to put into words, like that feeling of feeling like they must be, know something that I don't, or I must be doing something wrong, or there might be something wrong with me that I'm not, and just articulating that in words, even if it's been like a thought in the background for people when they read it, they're like, you know, oh my god, this person understands me, this person really gets me, this person, you know, and we all want to be understood. So there's a real element there. So when I first sat down to write the manifesto, not having any idea of how successful it was going to be, what really was my motivation though was to, I felt like I had a great product, and I felt that I was aware that I was getting people really great results. So those two things were there for me. And I had this limited amount of time that I needed to, that I wanted to do a coaching program just for a short period, which had not been the norm for me, because they had been like, when I had done it before, it was like 18 months long, and here I was going to do it in 11 weeks. But the problem was, is that I had no prospects for the coaching program at that moment. And so I wanted to write a document that would help people realize that they needed my product or service, because most people in the marketplace did not know that they needed anything, that they needed to know anything about business. So it's kind of like, you know, Todd and I shared, Todd found this document about pre-selling, and we went over it together, and it was a really valuable document. And the, and you know, this was later on, but in that document, what it was speaking about is that there's like, if you kind of imagine your buyers, like overall thoughts, emotions, feelings and everything, they're here, right? Like right now, that's where they are, that's kind of like what you document, your persona or an empathy map or anything like that. Like what is the ideal way for them to be in order to buy your product? Like what do they need to believe, what do they need to feel, what do they need to, etc. And that's kind of what my attempt in the Internet Business Manifesto was to do. Like if they knew these things, then they come to the same conclusion, right? And Mark Ford and I had a conversation late one night, because we like to theorize about like, come up with concepts, and we're talking about the marketing process as really, you know, if you believe certain things about your products, like your prospects must buy it, they really need it, so forth. And they don't. It's because you've had certain experiences that they haven't. You know, you've been led to that conclusion based on your life, they haven't had your life, so therefore they haven't been led to that conclusion. So marketing is really about compressing and accelerating your experience through them that led you to that same conclusion. So the manifesto for me was creating that kind of context where someone could come to the realization that their problem wasn't a marketing problem, but that marketing was part of the problem and the way they were approaching it, because they were being opportunistic. And I got the idea of that distinction from a call, right? Like I was listening to a call a year prior or six months prior or two years prior, I don't remember how long before, Jay Abraham was doing a series of calls to promote his strategy summit. And Jay, he was doing this one call with Paul Ember. And Jay made the distinction between strategy and tactics. And Paul corrected him and said, it's really not a question of strategy versus tactics, it's really a question of strategy versus opportunism. And that was like, that clicked for me, like immediately. See how being aware, right? Rich was aware of that one idea, that one concept, that he, ah, there's something to that, boom, and it's been worth multiple millions of dollars. One idea, being aware, paying attention, being intentional about that. Yeah, and I mean, and Paul's a business coach too, and a good one. But came out of his mouth and into my pocket. Talk about Joe Seinfeld, darn you. I'm sure he's probably like, business manifesto, mother. Yeah, like the, and I mean, I like, when I'm get interested in a topic, I read a lot, a tremendous amount. And one of the, you know, and I'm reading because I want to learn more, I want to know more, I want to understand more. But another thing I'm looking for is the way things are described and does it land for me. You know, there, there are concepts that I've learned that like when I learn them, I'm like, yeah, I got it. And then like three months later or four months later, like I come across it, said another way, or explained differently. And all of a sudden, the level of like depth that I get, it goes from like, you know, this much to like that much. And it was sometimes it's just because of where I was, personally, but a lot of the times it's the way it's articulated. And whenever I come across something like that, I like, I, I circle it, I take it out because it's like, okay, this is the way to describe that anything that like, I'm always taking the perspective that if it lands on me, it'll land on others, right. And so I consistently, anytime I come across something in a book that is powerful, like I'm like, wow, I put it to the side, anything that's describing a problem, you know, just anything, even it's outside my topic, just because who knows when I might want to use it, or sometimes it's just about looking at it and understanding why it worked. Why did it create an emotional response in me? So I consistently go back there. So anyway, yeah, the manifesto was really about getting people to see the problem differently. Get them to see that the real problem was not knowing enough about business itself, which was causing all these other problems to open up the need that what they really needed to learn was about business, not about more marketing. And you know, the results kind of speak for themselves. And immediately after that, like a week or two later, I wrote another report called The Missing Chapter. And I took the same, same approach, like I asked all, I asked readers why they wouldn't be successful, not that they knew what they knew in the manifesto. And they told me all these reasons. And when I boiled all these reasons down, like they couldn't get people to partner with them, they couldn't diss that the other, I said, all of those reasons stem from not building a business around your strength, not a place where you can easily provide value. And what and lo and behold, the second module in my program is all about discovering your strengths and building your business around it. So like there was a context for that. I kind of, I would say, I was surprised by the obviously, by the success of the manifesto, I had no idea that those 30 or so pages were going to radically change my life and they did. So then I immediately wrote more reports, because, hey, why not? But I kind of, I think all of the reports had big ideas. And we can go through them or what. But it wasn't until the last report I wrote that I really had kind of cracked a nut of what made the first one so successful. So there was, you know, the internet business manifesto, the missing chapter, the final chapter, the attention age doctrine one, the attention age doctrine two, the maven manifesto, and then the last one was the entrepreneurial emergency, the uncertainty syndrome. And it was really my first and my last that were the most successful. Because all through the other ones, I was trying to figure out what made the first one work so well. And what both of those have in common was that you think the problem is X, so you're doing Y, but the real problem is Z and why makes Z worse? If that kind of, you follow that logic. And so, you know, in the internet business manifesto, it was this distinction between opportunity seeker and strategic entrepreneur. And in the entrepreneurial emergency uncertainty syndrome, it was that you keep thinking increasing your potential is going to cause greater success when it's really what's stopping you from tapping your full potential now. Because adding more potential doesn't add to more success. You've already added a lot more potential and you're not more successful. So those two reports were different in the sense that they really kind of turned the perspective completely around and then offered a solution with that new perspective. It's crazy, right? I mean, it's unbelievable. You know, and it's, I would say, though, that the ideas come to me primarily based on the, what I said in the beginning, the position I'm taking, the cause and effect is a lot like it's not as obvious as it seems that people are struggling and therefore there's a solution that nobody sees that I will find. And then I'm going to go through everything that I go through looking for clues that support that, right? So that, you know, anything that supports this belief that everyone's looking at it wrong is part of what I'm gathering. So that by the time I'm done, I have a lot of proof, right? And it's really about, you know, it's like when I got diagnosed with ADD, my life didn't get any better, like at all. You know, it didn't occur to me at that moment that like, actually, like, I'm going to have that now. And I want to get rid of it. I got to take drugs or whatever. But in that moment, I felt like a tremendous amount of relief because it just was like, ah, no wonder, like, you know, and it really isn't my fault. Yeah. It really isn't. I couldn't have succeeded doing it that way. Right. And one of the things I've done, like in a webinar that I recorded a couple of years ago, was actually look at the things that people do that they don't like about themselves, right? Like staying up all night in front of the computer, you know, being kind of an info addict, those kinds of things. And positioning those as positive characteristics by looking at the positive intent. Every bad behavior has a positive intent, right? Like, we don't do anything without a reason. Even taking drugs, like, you know, there's a positive intent there. And looking at the positive intent, showing them that the positive intent is actually a driver of success, but just misapplied. Right. So I think I have it here. You want to take that? There are a couple things like a draw to that would make. So, you know, I took, like, unsure which marketing tactics to use, right? Like, that's what they're feeling. Well, the positive trait behind that, because I didn't call it positive intent, because that would have been a little bit too transparent. Trying to find and use best practices, that's a good thing, right? Wasting more and more of my life online means that underneath that, you have a trait of willing to do whatever it takes to succeed. So what I'm doing at them in this is kind of working on the self-esteem of the prospect, making them feel better about who they are, and making the method, like, the way they've been trying to achieve success, really the guilty party in it. Because I'm a big believer in another thing that Dan Kennedy, you know, Todd's mentioned him a couple times. Another thing that Dan Kennedy says, which is that, you know, the biggest problem oftentimes in marketing is the self-esteem of your prospect, like, the belief that they can't do it. Like, you've been successful, you might have even taught a lot of people to be successful, but if you knew my story and you knew how messed up I was, you would know that I couldn't succeed. So I'm taking that as a given. I'm taking it that, like, most people who I'm speaking to feel that way about themselves, and so I need to kind of hit that, hit that button, so to speak, and get them to get off of their sob story and thinking that they're flawed and get them to see that the flaw has never been in them, but it's been in the method that they have been applying. It's a huge... Like, the overwhelm, basically. Well, yeah, I mean, it's like, so if someone's feeling overwhelmed, you know, and the first thing is, is that what I would say is like, in fiction writing, they talk a lot about, like, show, don't tell, right? So, you know, what does that overwhelm look like, right? Because overwhelm is just too generic, right, as a phrase, right? Like, because I don't... Like, if I feel, if I really feel overwhelmed and you just say overwhelmed, I don't know that you really get me, right? But if you talk about, like, how that overwhelm shows up, like, now I know that you know what I'm really feeling, and then speak to that overwhelm from a standpoint of what's causing it, is this desire to find, like, the answer, right? Or the method that's going to work for you, or whatever it is that's causing the overwhelm, right? Do you get that? Yeah, is it more in the context of, like, an aspirational messaging or more... Really speaking to that overwhelm, what does that really look like? Well, you follow? No, go... I have an answer, but I don't think it's the answer to your question. I mean, what is... What is the messaging look like going up there? The messaging is, like, well, I don't think this is going to answer your question, but maybe it'll... I think he's asking, like, how do you demonstrate that you do understand their overwhelm? Right, without saying straight out, I know you're overwhelmed, but here's where I'm going to take you. Are you saying it just like that? Are you saying it in a very... Well, I get, you know, I might be saying something like that. I get that you might feel overwhelmed, right? That you might look at... So you're throwing it out there. But I say that, but then I back it up with, like, you might start your day with 75 things that you know you need to do, that need to get done for you to be successful, and there's just absolutely no way that you're going to get more than 10 of them done. That by the... You know that when your head hits the pillow that night, there's going to be at least 65 things still left undone, and tomorrow, not only will you have those 65, but new things will have showed up. See how he, first of all, he painted a picture of experience and it's concrete. It's not a random overwhelm, but it's a concrete specific experience that he painted a picture of. He demonstrated it through his works. It's much people relate to that, obviously. Yeah, yeah. And then it's like, and then say, and I totally get why that shows up for you, because you're totally committed and you're looking for the right way to do things, but what if I could show you a way that didn't have 65 things to do, but only had five things to do? What would happen to your overwhelm and how would you feel waking up knowing that not only were you not... Forget about not getting it all done, but you were going to get it all done by noon, and how different you feel waking up in the morning, like kind of pacing that. And so it paints an entirely different picture for the prospect, right? And the... I don't know if this is helpful, but I created this like a long time ago. There are a couple of things I created. Can I draw this? Sure. But it's like three circles, and this is the problem. Like what they think the problem is. This is their desires. These are the prospects. This is what I'm labeling like the root cause of the problem. So where it overlaps here is symptoms. Where it overlaps here is the solution. Where it overlaps here is the ideal buying state, like what they need to believe, what they need to feel. And then right in here is your product. I don't know if this confuses people or makes it easier to understand, but you know they have a problem. They have desires where that overlaps being familiar enough with their problem and being familiar enough with their desires like kind of creates the ideal buying state. This root cause of the problem is just one way that I get to problems, right? So it's the underlying problem. Whatever the problem is that you're going to address, that on top of their desires, it provides the solution that on top of the problem shows that the problems are really symptoms. And so your like what you're offering is right in the middle, kind of is taking it all their ideal buying state, having their problems show up as symptoms and having a new solution, you know, based on that. So that where all them overlap is right there. I don't know if that... Yeah, that's awesome. The... See what else I have here, like things that I just want to make sure I cover and you don't ask me anything. Sure. The beautiful part of... I guess, well, so let me before I get there. So there's a couple of ways that I've kind of developed these kind of ideas. One is, you know, just ask myself the question, what would they need to know or believe and feel to make my product seem like a must have item. Another way is doing like a root cause analysis, which, you know, like since I have studied a lot of crazy things, I do it with theory of constraints, but you could just do it by keep asking why and why and why to all their problems and just take it deeper and deeper. And the idea with any kind of root cause analysis is that you start with a bunch of problems. And when you're asked why enough, they start to converge, right? So it's a convergence of costs, which is trying to find the root, the core of it that kind of expands out to all the problems. Show you the power of this just as a quick aside. And if maybe we have a minute or two, I'll let you share. So what he was just talking about, the theory of constraints. How many of you have done any kind of studying a theory of constraints? Yeah, pretty much. That's what I thought. And so the power, he was able to take this theory of constraints and introduce it to the online marketing community and make a bundle of money by coming up with the right idea behind it, not introducing it as theory of constraints, not introducing it as, but totally repositioning, totally just amazing. Amazing how you can, amazing. And that's the whole concept of that it's not your potential that finds your success and what's blocking your potential because it's a constraint. Another couple of ways that I've come up with them is going backwards from the solution, right, which is kind of what do they need to believe. Another way is to sell against a sales letter. Like if you take a common sales letter in your marketplace and then sell against it, like how would that be the problem? And I find that that's actually a creative way to kind of get it to the answer, right, like just take any sales letter that works really well and your job is to sell that as the problem. Like this letter here represents everything that's bad, right, and why is it bad? There's something called like chunking up and chunking down from NLP. So it's like Porsche is a type of sports car, which is a type of car, which is like a vehicle of transportation, right. And so the idea is looking at different levels of their problems, like what is it a function of going up, what is the function going down, and then also looking at your solution, like it's a function going up, going down, just to kind of get at different ways to kind of probe, because you know, kind of along the lines of what Todd was saying, it's like I believe that there's an answer out there, like I know there is. I know there's a way to kind of position this product that is going to explode in people's minds. And I just have to find it. So I'm going to like try all these different things until I come across something that like, oh, that's it. That's sexy. That's the thing. That's the thing that everybody's going to get. And now how do you know when you've come to that? Like how do you, I told these guys earlier about the whole, your whole this changes everything, right. And how you still like when we work together, you would be like, tell me how this changes everything. And how do you, how do you know when you have that type of idea? Well, I'd say that oftentimes I feel like I have that idea, but then it's still way too complicated. Right. Like, like I could explain it, but I need like three pages to explain it. And, and that's just, I think you just get a visceral kind of feeling like this is it, this is sexy, this is like, I got it. But then for me, it's about having a lot of conversations with people, trying to describe it, trying to work through it, you know, and Todd's always on the receiving end of that. It's almost like as new to you as it is to the other people, right? I mean, yeah, yeah, it is. And, you know, one of the things that Mark Ford said to me years ago, that he liked about my reports, and it wasn't like a copywriting trick, because I'm not really a copywriter. So it was, it was pure and true. But as a tactic, it's very powerful is that I was like, I the, the tone and like the voice and the energy behind the writing of the manifesto and all my reports is of someone who just discovered a secret that can't wait to share it. Right. And, you know, there's an element of that, like, in creating or delivering counterintuitive content. It's a form of social currency. You know, if you've ever had a secret and really wanted to share it, the reason you want to share it is, is that it's a form of social currency. By sharing it, you become more interesting. And so if you are coming from that place and you actually deliver something of that, then that makes them want to share it, right? They become smarter, whatever, more appealing in whatever way, because now they know something that I was done. And so that kind of creates that desire to pass along stuff. And it's always generally in the counterintuitive stuff that does that the best. Like, if you ever come across something that was such a juxtaposition, you were looking forward to sharing it with someone like in your marketplace or someone who shared your passion, because all of a sudden you had a new way of looking at it that they hadn't heard of, which makes you excited.
